description: The figure presents strain hardening rate versus true stress curves for an austenitic high-Mn low-density 8Al steel (Fe–30.5Mn–8.0Al–1.2C wt.%) tensile tested at an initial strain rate of $5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{s}^{-1}}$. Two heat treatment conditions are compared: short annealing at 450°C for 1 h (black curve) and long annealing at 600°C for 24 h (red curve). Both specimens exhibit an initial strain hardening rate of approximately 5 GPa. The short-annealed sample shows a conventional three-stage hardening behavior with a stable plateau around 2 GPa between 800–1100 MPa before declining. In contrast, the long-annealed sample displays a sharp drop in hardening rate to ~2 GPa at ~1150 MPa, followed by a distinct upward inflection to ~2.2 GPa at higher stresses (>1400 MPa). These contrasting behaviors reflect different precipitation states: short annealing produces heterogeneously distributed shearable nanosized κ-carbides (2–5 nm) enabling planar slip and sustained work hardening, whereas long annealing creates a uniform dispersion of cuboidal κ-carbides (10–20 nm) with narrow matrix channels, resulting in higher strength but reduced ductility.
